name: Configure and use Secret Protection
tagline: Stop credentials at the push, catch the ones already in history, and prioritise by whether the secret is still live. Enable it, tune it, and know who may push past a block — and what 'resolved' really means.
overview: One of the three heaviest domains on GH-500 (15–20%). The July-2026 refresh renamed the suite: the product is now GitHub Secret Protection (the detection engine inside it is still called secret scanning). This domain tests whether you can enable Secret Protection at repository and organisation scope; use push protection to block a secret before it ever lands in history; understand validity checks and the prioritised, high-confidence alerts they drive; work the alert lifecycle (and know that closing an alert does not rotate the credential); create custom patterns for org-specific secrets; and configure who may bypass a push block through role-based and delegated bypass. The recurring exam instinct: prevention-first (block at the source) beats clean-up, and the human accountable for a secret is the one who rotates it — not the one who closes the alert.
objectives: Enable GitHub Secret Protection at the repository and organisation levels, and contrast its behaviour for public versus private/enterprise repositories.
Explain how push protection prevents secrets at the source, and how that differs from detecting secrets already committed to history.
Describe validity checks and how confirmed-active secrets drive prioritised, high-confidence alerting.
Work the Secret Protection alert lifecycle — creation, status, dismissal — and remediate correctly (rotate the secret; a closed alert is not a rotated credential).
Configure role-based and delegated bypass for push protection, alert recipients and exclusions, and custom secret patterns.

lessons.title: Enable Secret Protection: the product, the engine, and where it runs
lessons.objective: After this you can enable GitHub Secret Protection at repo and org scope and explain how coverage differs for public vs private/enterprise repositories.
lessons.body:
## Two names, one job

The July-2026 refresh renamed the suite, and the exam leans on the distinction:

- **GitHub Secret Protection** is the *product* (the licensable feature set). It is what you enable, and what a private or internal repository needs a licence for.
- **secret scanning** is the *detection engine* inside it — the thing that actually scans for exposed credentials. GitHub docs still use the term "secret scanning" for the mechanism.

> **Currency trap:** on the exam, the *product/domain* name is **Secret Protection** (formerly "secret scanning"). Lead with "Secret Protection"; the word "secret scanning" is now the name of the underlying capability, not the product. A distractor that calls the *product* "secret scanning" is testing exactly this.

## Where it runs — public vs private/enterprise

This is a favourite exam fork:

- **Public repositories:** secret scanning **runs automatically, for free** — you don't buy anything. GitHub scans the full Git history on all branches.
- **Private and internal repositories:** you need **GitHub Secret Protection enabled** on **GitHub Team** or **GitHub Enterprise Cloud** (or GitHub Enterprise Server with Secret Protection enabled). This is a paid product.

So "why isn't my private repo being scanned?" is almost always "Secret Protection isn't enabled / licensed here" — not a config bug.

## Enabling it — repo and org

- **Repository level:** a repo admin turns on Secret Protection in **Settings → Advanced Security** (or Code security), enabling secret scanning and, separately, **push protection**.
- **Organisation level:** an org owner enables it across repositories — typically through a **security configuration** applied to repos, so new and existing repos inherit the setting instead of being toggled one by one. Enterprise owners can push defaults down further still.

The mental model to carry into the exam: **enterprise → organisation → repository**, with defaults inheriting downward. You rarely want to click 500 repos by hand; you apply a configuration and let it inherit. (Enterprise-scale rollout and inheritance is D6's territory — here, know that repo and org are both valid enable points and org-level is how you scale.)

## What detection actually covers

Once enabled, secret scanning looks for:

- **Partner patterns** — credentials for known providers (cloud keys, tokens). When a partner secret leaks in a public repo, GitHub can **notify the provider** so they can revoke it. You don't configure these; they ship built-in.
- **Non-provider patterns** — generic secrets not tied to one provider: private keys, connection strings, generic API keys.
- **Custom patterns** — your own regexes for org-specific secrets (covered in Lesson 4).

## Why the exam cares

The blueprint's first D2 group is literally *"Enable and configure Secret Protection."* Expect questions that (a) test the Secret Protection vs "secret scanning" naming, (b) hand you a public-vs-private availability fork, and (c) ask where you enable it to cover a whole org efficiently. Anchor on: **public = free & automatic; private/internal = needs Secret Protection; scale via org/enterprise configuration, not per-repo clicks.**

knowledgeCheck.stem: A team pushes code to a **public** repository and asks how to turn on secret scanning. What's the correct answer?

options.text: Nothing to buy or enable — secret scanning runs automatically and free on public repositories
They must purchase GitHub Secret Protection before any scanning happens
Secret scanning only works on private repositories
They must first push a secret so GitHub knows what to scan for

knowledgeCheck.explanation: On **public** repositories, secret scanning runs automatically at no cost. GitHub Secret Protection is the paid product needed for **private/internal** repos (b is the private-repo case). Scanning covers public repos too, so (c) is wrong, and detection is pattern-based — it never needs you to leak a secret first (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: You need Secret Protection on for **every** repository in an organisation, including ones created next month, without visiting each repo. What's the right approach?

options.text: Enable it at the organisation level via a security configuration so repositories inherit the setting
Open every repository's settings and toggle it one at a time
Make all the repositories public so scanning is automatic
Ask each developer to enable it locally in their Git client

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Org-level enablement via a **security configuration** applies the setting across repos and lets new repos inherit it — the scalable control. Per-repo toggling (b) doesn't cover future repos; making repos public (c) is a security disaster to save a licence; and Secret Protection is a server-side GitHub feature, not a local Git setting (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: On the July-2026 exam, which statement about the naming is correct?

options.text: 'GitHub Secret Protection' is the product; 'secret scanning' is the detection engine inside it
'Secret scanning' is the current product name and 'Secret Protection' was the old one
They are unrelated features that must both be licensed separately
'Secret Protection' only refers to push protection, nothing else

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The refresh made **Secret Protection** the product name; **secret scanning** remains the name of the underlying detection capability. The direction in (b) is reversed. They aren't unrelated (c) — one contains the other. And Secret Protection is the whole suite (detection + push protection + custom patterns + validity checks), not push protection alone (d).

lessons.title: Push protection: block the secret before it lands
lessons.objective: After this you can explain how push protection prevents secrets at the source, how that differs from detecting already-committed secrets, and what happens when a push is blocked.
lessons.body:
## Prevention vs detection — the core split

Secret Protection works two ways, and the exam wants you to keep them straight:

- **Detection (alerts):** secret scanning scans your **existing** Git history — all branches — and raises an **alert** when it finds a credential that's *already committed*. This is clean-up: the secret is in history, and now you must rotate it.
- **Push protection (prevention):** when a contributor tries to `git push` a commit that contains a **supported secret**, GitHub **blocks the push before it reaches the repository**. The credential never enters history in the first place.

> **The one-liner to memorise:** *push protection stops a secret from ever being committed; alerts tell you about a secret that already was.* Prevention-first beats clean-up because a pushed secret must be treated as compromised even after you delete it.

## What a blocked push looks like

The contributor gets a detailed message naming the secret it detected. They then choose:

1. **Remove the secret and push again** — the intended path. Take the credential out of the commit (and ideally move it to a secrets manager), then re-push. Clean.
2. **Bypass the block** — if they believe it's safe or must proceed, they give a **reason**. What they pick changes what GitHub records:

| Bypass reason | What GitHub does |
|---|---|
| **It's used in tests** | Creates a **closed** alert, resolved as *used in tests* |
| **It's a false positive** | Creates a **closed** alert, resolved as *false positive* |
| **I'll fix it later** | Creates an **open** alert (so it stays on the radar) |

Notice the design: even a bypass leaves a **trail**. "I'll fix it later" deliberately leaves an **open** alert so the debt is visible and someone follows up.

## Who can bypass — and the default gotcha

**By default, anyone with write access to the repository can bypass push protection** by specifying a reason. That's the exam trap: out of the box, push protection is a **speed bump**, not a hard wall — any writer can push past it. To make it a real gate, you restrict who may bypass with **delegated bypass** (Lesson 4).

## Custom patterns get push protection too

Push protection isn't limited to GitHub's built-in patterns. You can **enable push protection for a custom pattern**, so your org-specific secret formats are blocked at the push just like partner secrets. (Custom patterns are Lesson 4.)

## Why the exam cares

The blueprint's *"Prevent secret exposure"* group leads with *"Explain Push Protection and how it prevents secrets at the source."* Expect scenario questions that contrast push protection with detection ("the secret is already in history — does push protection help?" → no, that's an alert/rotation problem), that test the three bypass reasons and their outcomes, and that probe the default "any writer can bypass" behaviour. Anchor on: **push protection = prevention at the push; bypass always leaves a trail; the default is permissive until you delegate.**

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does push protection do that alert-based detection does not?

options.text: It blocks a commit containing a supported secret before it ever reaches the repository
It scans existing history and opens alerts for secrets already committed
It automatically rotates any leaked credential for you
It deletes the repository if a secret is found

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Push protection is **prevention** — it stops the secret at the push, before it enters history. Scanning existing history and opening alerts (b) is the *detection* side, the thing push protection complements. GitHub never rotates your credentials for you (c) — that's your job — and it certainly doesn't delete repos (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: A developer is blocked by push protection and selects **"I'll fix it later"** to proceed. What happens?

options.text: GitHub allows the push and creates an **open** secret scanning alert so the issue stays tracked
GitHub allows the push and creates a **closed** alert, considering it resolved
GitHub silently allows the push with no record at all
GitHub permanently blocks that developer from the repository

knowledgeCheck.explanation: "I'll fix it later" leaves an **open** alert on purpose — the secret is now in history and the debt must stay visible. The *used in tests* and *false positive* reasons create **closed** alerts (b describes those, not this one). A bypass never happens without a record (c), and it doesn't ban the user (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: With only the **default** settings, who can bypass a push-protection block?

options.text: Anyone with write access to the repository, by giving a bypass reason
Only organisation owners and security managers
Nobody — push protection can never be bypassed
Only the person who originally enabled push protection

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Out of the box, **any user with write access** can bypass by specifying a reason — push protection defaults to permissive. Restricting bypass to owners/security managers or a curated list (b) is what **delegated bypass** does *after* you configure it. It's not un-bypassable (c), and it isn't tied to whoever enabled it (d).

lessons.title: Validity checks and the alert lifecycle: 'resolved' is not 'rotated'
lessons.objective: After this you can explain how validity checks drive prioritised alerts, walk the alert lifecycle, and remediate correctly — knowing that closing an alert does not revoke the credential.
lessons.body:
## Validity checks — is this secret still live?

A detected secret is scarier if it still works. **Validity checks** ask the secret's provider whether the credential is **still active**, so you can triage by real risk instead of treating every hit equally. GitHub reports a validity status of:

- **active** — confirmed still valid (highest priority; rotate now)
- **inactive** — confirmed no longer valid
- **unknown** — couldn't be determined

You enable validity checks in the repo/org/enterprise **security settings** ("automatically verify if a secret is valid"). GitHub then **periodically** re-checks, and you can also run an **on-demand** re-check from the alert view. To be minimally intrusive, GitHub typically makes read-only (GET) calls to the least sensitive endpoint.

### Validity → prioritised alerts

This is the exam link the blueprint spells out (*"validity checks and prioritized alerting for high-confidence secrets"*): a secret **confirmed active** is a **high-confidence, high-priority** alert. Validity lets a security team attack the credentials that are provably exploitable *first*, rather than wading through everything. **Prioritisation is driven by validity, not by detection order.**

## The alert lifecycle

A secret scanning alert moves through simple states:

1. **Open** — created when secret scanning finds a credential (in history, or via a "I'll fix it later" bypass).
2. **Closed / resolved** — a person closes it with a **reason** (e.g. *revoked*, *false positive*, *used in tests*, *won't fix*). Some bypass paths auto-create a **closed** alert (used in tests, false positive).

> **The most-tested trap in this domain:** *closing an alert does NOT revoke, rotate, or remove the secret.* GitHub even notes that secret scanning **doesn't automatically close an alert when the token is removed from the repository — you must close it manually** — which underlines that alert state and credential state are two different things. An alert marked *revoked* is a human **claiming** they revoked it; GitHub didn't do it for them.

## Remediate correctly

The order that earns credit:

1. **Revoke/rotate** the exposed credential at the provider (delete the old one, create a new one). Assume anything committed is compromised — even if you later rewrite history.
2. **Update** every service that used the old secret to the new one.
3. **Check logs** for unauthorised use while it was exposed.
4. **Then** close the alert with the right reason — *as bookkeeping*, after the real fix.

Deleting the commit or force-pushing history is **not** remediation on its own: the secret was exposed, may be cached or cloned, and must be rotated regardless.

## Dismissing alerts responsibly

The blueprint calls out *"implications and best practices for dismissing or ignoring alerts."* Dismissing a *real* live secret as "won't fix" or "false positive" to clear the queue is exactly the anti-pattern: it hides a genuine exposure. That risk is *why* orgs add **delegated alert dismissal** — requiring a reviewer (a security-manager team) to approve dismissals so alerts aren't closed carelessly (Lesson 4).

## Why the exam cares

The *"Manage and respond to Secret Protection alerts"* group is a whole section of the blueprint. Expect: validity-status questions (which alert do you work first? → the **active** one), lifecycle-state questions, and — above all — the *resolved ≠ rotated* trap. Anchor on: **validity drives priority; remediation means rotating the secret; closing an alert is bookkeeping, not a fix.**

knowledgeCheck.stem: Two secret scanning alerts land at once. Validity checks report one secret **active** and the other **inactive**. Which do you work first, and why?

options.text: The **active** one — a confirmed-live credential is the higher-confidence, higher-priority risk
The **inactive** one — inactive means it needs re-enabling first
Whichever was detected first; validity is irrelevant to priority
Neither — validity checks close alerts automatically

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Validity checks exist to prioritise: an **active** secret is provably exploitable, so it's the high-priority alert. An **inactive** secret can't currently be abused (b misreads "inactive"). Priority is driven by **validity, not detection order** (c), and validity checks inform triage — they don't auto-close alerts (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: A developer force-pushes to delete the commit that leaked an API key, then closes the secret scanning alert as "revoked." What's the problem?

options.text: Deleting the commit and closing the alert don't revoke the key — it was exposed and must actually be rotated at the provider
Nothing — removing the commit fully remediates the leak
Closing an alert automatically rotates the credential, so it's fine
Force-pushing is the only valid remediation for any secret

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The alert state and the credential state are independent: a leaked secret may already be cloned or cached, so it must be **rotated at the provider** regardless of history rewrites. Removing the commit is not remediation (b), closing an alert never rotates anything (c), and force-pushing is not a remediation step at all — rotation is (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does closing a secret scanning alert as "revoked" actually do in GitHub?

options.text: It records your resolution on the alert; you still had to revoke the secret yourself at the provider
It calls the provider and revokes the credential on your behalf
It removes the secret from all past commits
It re-scans the whole repository from scratch

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Closing an alert is **bookkeeping** — it records that a human resolved it; GitHub does not revoke the credential (b) or scrub history (c) for you, and closing an alert doesn't trigger a fresh scan (d). This is the domain's signature trap: *resolved is not rotated.*

lessons.title: Custom patterns, delegated bypass, and access controls
lessons.objective: After this you can create custom secret patterns, configure role-based and delegated bypass so push protection becomes a real gate, and manage alert recipients and delegated dismissal.
lessons.body:
## Custom patterns — catch *your* secrets

GitHub's built-in partner and non-provider patterns don't know your internal token formats. **Custom patterns** are **regular expressions you define** to detect org-specific secrets. Key exam facts:

- You can define them at the **repository, organisation, or enterprise** level (org/enterprise patterns apply across many repos).
- You author a pattern with a **secret format** regex, and can add **before/after** context and **additional match / must-not-match** requirements to cut false positives.
- After defining a pattern you run a **dry run** to see what it *would* match before it goes live — so you don't flood the org with noise.
- Crucially, **you can enable push protection on a custom pattern** — so your internal secret formats are blocked at the push, exactly like partner secrets.

Custom patterns are a **Secret Protection / GHAS** capability (the paid product), not something public repos get for free.

## Delegated bypass — turn the speed bump into a gate

Recall the default: **any writer can bypass** a push-protection block. **Delegated bypass** is how you lock that down.

- You grant **bypass permissions** to selected **individuals, roles, and teams** — a **bypass list**. Members of it (plus **organisation owners** and **security managers**) can push past a block directly.
- **Everyone else** who hits a block must **request** a bypass, which goes into a **review cycle**. A reviewer with bypass privileges **approves or denies** it. Requests **expire after 7 days** if not actioned.
- You can also grant an **exemption** — an actor who skips push protection entirely for all their commits (use sparingly; e.g. a trusted automation).

> **Exam framing:** delegated bypass is *who may push past a block, and who reviews the request.* Privileged users bypass directly; unprivileged users are **subject to review**. This is what makes push protection an enforced gate rather than an honour system.

## Delegated *alert dismissal* — the mirror control for alerts

There's a parallel control on the alert side. **Delegated alert dismissal** requires that closing/dismissing a secret scanning alert be **reviewed and approved** (typically by a **team of security managers**) instead of any developer closing it unilaterally. It's enabled at repo/org/enterprise scope (org/enterprise via a security configuration). The point: stop real live secrets from being quietly dismissed as "false positive" or "won't fix." Ensure the reviewer team has enough coverage to keep up.

> Don't confuse the two: **delegated *bypass*** governs pushing past a **push-protection block** (prevention side); **delegated *dismissal*** governs closing an **alert** (detection side). Both add a reviewer; they act at different points.

## Alert recipients and exclusions

The blueprint lists *"Configure alert recipients and exclusions."* You can control **who is notified** of secret scanning alerts (org/enterprise owners, security managers, repo admins are notified on push-protection bypass, for instance) and define **exclusions** — paths or files (e.g. via `secret_scanning.yml`) that scanning should ignore, so known test fixtures don't generate perpetual noise. Exclusions cut false positives; use them narrowly so you don't blind yourself to real secrets.

## Why the exam cares

The *"Control access, policies, and customization"* group ties this lesson together: **role-based and delegated bypass**, **alert recipients and exclusions**, and **custom patterns**. Expect: custom-pattern questions (levels, dry run, push protection on custom patterns), delegated-bypass questions (who bypasses directly vs who is reviewed; the 7-day expiry), and the **delegated bypass vs delegated dismissal** distinction. Anchor on: **custom patterns extend detection (and push protection) to your secrets; delegated bypass/dismissal add a reviewer so neither pushing-past nor closing is a free-for-all.**

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your company issues internal tokens in a format GitHub doesn't recognise, and you want them **blocked at the push**. What do you do?

options.text: Create a custom pattern (a regex) for the token format and enable push protection on it
File a support ticket asking GitHub to add your format as a partner pattern
Nothing is possible — push protection only works on GitHub's built-in patterns
Make the repository public so scanning covers custom formats automatically

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A **custom pattern** defines the format via regex, and you can **enable push protection on it** to block those secrets at the push. Partner patterns are for GitHub's provider partners, not a per-customer request (b); push protection explicitly supports custom patterns (c); and going public exposes your code without adding custom detection (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: With delegated bypass configured, a developer **not** on the bypass list is blocked by push protection and needs to proceed. What happens?

options.text: They submit a bypass request that a privileged reviewer must approve or deny; it expires after 7 days if unactioned
They can still bypass immediately by picking any reason, as before
Their write access is automatically revoked
The repository is locked until an admin intervenes

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Delegated bypass puts unprivileged contributors into a **review cycle**: a request is raised and a reviewer with bypass privileges approves or denies it, expiring after **7 days**. Immediate self-bypass (b) is the *default* behaviour delegated bypass removes. It doesn't revoke access (c) or lock the repo (d).

knowledgeCheck.stem: A security lead wants two guarantees: (1) developers can't silently close real secret alerts as 'false positive', and (2) only a trusted group can push past a push-protection block. Which controls deliver these?

options.text: (1) delegated alert dismissal; (2) delegated bypass for push protection
Both are handled by delegated bypass alone
(1) custom patterns; (2) validity checks
Both are handled by making the repositories private

knowledgeCheck.explanation: These are two different reviewer controls: **delegated alert dismissal** requires approval to close an alert (guarantee 1), while **delegated bypass** restricts and reviews who may push past a block (guarantee 2). One control can't do both (b) because they act at different points — dismissal on alerts, bypass on pushes. Custom patterns and validity checks (c) are detection tools, not access controls, and repo visibility (d) governs neither.

questions.stem: As of the July-2026 exam, what is the current **product** name for GitHub's secret-detection suite?

options.text: GitHub Secret Protection
GitHub Secret Scanning
GitHub Dependabot
GitHub Code Security

questions.explanation: The refresh renamed the product to **Secret Protection** (the detection engine inside it is still called *secret scanning*). "Secret scanning" is the legacy product term now used for the mechanism (b). Dependabot is the old name for Supply Chain Security (c); Code Security is the CodeQL suite (d).

questions.stem: Secret scanning runs automatically and free on which repositories?

options.text: Public repositories
Private repositories with no licence
Only repositories owned by enterprises
Only forks of public repositories

questions.explanation: **Public** repositories get secret scanning automatically at no cost. Private/internal repos need **GitHub Secret Protection** (a paid product), so (b) is wrong. It isn't limited to enterprise-owned repos (c) or to forks (d).

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of **push protection**?

options.text: To block a commit containing a supported secret before it enters the repository
To scan existing history for previously committed secrets
To automatically rotate leaked credentials
To require two-factor authentication for pushes

questions.explanation: Push protection is **prevention at the push** — it stops the secret before it's committed. Scanning existing history (b) is the detection/alert side. GitHub never rotates credentials for you (c), and push protection is unrelated to 2FA (d).

questions.stem: A secret was committed to `main` three weeks ago and is now in history. Does push protection help with *this* secret?

options.text: No — push protection only blocks new pushes; this already-committed secret is an alert-and-rotate problem
Yes — push protection retroactively removes it from history
Yes — push protection rotates it at the provider automatically
No — nothing can detect a secret once it's committed

questions.explanation: Push protection acts **at push time**; a secret already in history is handled by **detection** (an alert) and must be **rotated**. Push protection doesn't rewrite history (b) or rotate credentials (c). And secret scanning absolutely *does* detect already-committed secrets (d) — that's the alerts side.

questions.stem: A developer bypasses a push-protection block with the reason **"It's a false positive."** What does GitHub record?

options.text: A closed alert resolved as "false positive"
An open alert that stays on the radar
No record at all
A support ticket to GitHub

questions.explanation: "False positive" and "used in tests" bypasses create a **closed** alert with that resolution. An **open** alert (b) is what "I'll fix it later" produces. A bypass always leaves a record (c), and it doesn't open a GitHub support ticket (d).

questions.stem: By default (no delegated bypass), who can bypass a push-protection block?

options.text: Anyone with write access to the repository
Only the repository owner
Only GitHub staff
No one

questions.explanation: The default is permissive: **any user with write access** can bypass by giving a reason. It isn't limited to the owner (b) or GitHub staff (c), and it is definitely bypassable by default (d) — restricting it is what delegated bypass adds.

questions.stem: What do **validity checks** tell you about a detected secret?

options.text: Whether the credential is still active (valid) with its provider
Which developer originally committed it
How to rotate it automatically
Whether the repository is public or private

questions.explanation: Validity checks verify with the provider whether the secret is **still active** (active / inactive / unknown), so you can prioritise live credentials. They don't attribute authorship (b), perform rotation (c), or report repo visibility (d).

questions.stem: Why do validity checks lead to "prioritised" alerts?

options.text: A secret confirmed **active** is a high-confidence, exploitable risk you should remediate first
Alerts are prioritised strictly by the order secrets were detected
Active secrets are lower risk than inactive ones
Validity checks delete low-priority alerts

questions.explanation: An **active** secret is provably usable, so it's the high-priority, high-confidence alert — that's the prioritisation. It's driven by validity, **not detection order** (b). Active is *higher* risk than inactive, not lower (c), and validity checks inform triage rather than deleting alerts (d).

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly remediates a leaked, still-active API key?

options.text: Revoke/rotate the key at the provider, update services to the new key, check logs, then close the alert
Close the alert as 'revoked'; GitHub rotates the key for you
Force-push to delete the commit; that fully remediates it
Mark it 'won't fix' and move on

questions.explanation: Real remediation is **rotate → update → verify → then close the alert as bookkeeping**. Closing an alert never rotates the key (b); deleting the commit doesn't undo the exposure (c); and dismissing a live secret as 'won't fix' hides a genuine risk (d).

questions.stem: GitHub notes that secret scanning does **not** automatically close an alert when the token is removed from the repository. What's the practical lesson?

options.text: Alert state and credential state are independent — you close alerts manually, and closing one doesn't revoke the secret
Removing a token from a file is all you ever need to do
GitHub auto-closes alerts as soon as history is rewritten
Alerts can never be closed

questions.explanation: The behaviour underscores that **alert state ≠ credential state**: you must close alerts manually, and closing is not revocation. Removing the token from a file isn't full remediation (b); GitHub explicitly does *not* auto-close on history changes (c); and alerts certainly can be closed — manually (d).

questions.stem: What are **custom patterns** in Secret Protection?

options.text: User-defined regular expressions that detect org-specific secrets not covered by built-in patterns
Machine-learning models GitHub trains on your repository
Pre-built partner patterns for known cloud providers
A list of files to exclude from scanning

questions.explanation: Custom patterns are **regexes you author** for your own secret formats. They aren't trained ML models (b); partner patterns are the built-in provider ones (c); and an exclusion list is a different feature (d).

questions.stem: At which levels can you define custom secret-scanning patterns?

options.text: Repository, organisation, or enterprise
Repository only
Enterprise only
Only in a developer's local Git config

questions.explanation: Custom patterns can be created at the **repository, organisation, or enterprise** level, so org/enterprise patterns apply across many repos. It isn't repo-only (b) or enterprise-only (c), and it's a server-side GitHub feature, not a local Git setting (d).

questions.stem: After writing a custom pattern, what's the recommended step before relying on it?

options.text: Run a dry run to preview what it would match, then refine to reduce false positives
Immediately enable push protection on it with no testing
Delete all built-in patterns so they don't conflict
Make the repository public to validate the regex

questions.explanation: A **dry run** shows what the pattern *would* match so you can tune it before it generates alerts org-wide. Enabling push protection before testing risks blocking developers on a noisy pattern (b); you never delete built-in patterns (c); and repo visibility has nothing to do with validating a regex (d).

questions.stem: What does **delegated bypass** for push protection change?

options.text: It restricts who can push past a block and routes other contributors' bypass requests for review/approval
It disables push protection entirely
It automatically rotates any secret that is bypassed
It makes every alert a false positive

questions.explanation: Delegated bypass turns the permissive default into a **gate**: only a bypass list (plus owners/security managers) bypasses directly; everyone else is **reviewed**. It doesn't disable push protection (b), rotate secrets (c), or touch alert classification (d).

questions.stem: Under delegated bypass, how long does an unactioned bypass **request** remain before it expires?

options.text: 7 days
24 hours
30 days
It never expires

questions.explanation: Bypass requests expire after **7 days** if a reviewer hasn't approved or denied them. The other durations are incorrect, and requests do expire — they don't linger forever (d).

questions.stem: Which pair correctly distinguishes **delegated bypass** from **delegated alert dismissal**?

options.text: Bypass reviews who may push past a push-protection block; dismissal reviews who may close a secret scanning alert
They are the same feature with two names
Bypass governs closing alerts; dismissal governs pushing past blocks
Both only apply to public repositories

questions.explanation: They're two reviewer controls acting at different points: **bypass** on the *prevention* side (pushing past a block), **dismissal** on the *detection* side (closing an alert). They aren't the same feature (b), and (c) swaps their meanings. Neither is limited to public repos (d).

questions.stem: Even with delegated bypass configured, which roles can typically bypass push protection **without** submitting a request for review?

options.text: Organisation owners, security managers, and members of the configured bypass list
Any user with read access
Only GitHub support staff
Nobody — all bypasses require review once delegated bypass is on

questions.explanation: Privileged actors — **org owners, security managers, and the bypass list** — bypass directly; everyone else is reviewed. Read-only users can't push at all (b); it's not restricted to GitHub staff (c); and privileged users are exactly the ones *not* subject to review (d).

questions.stem: A repo has a test fixture with a fake token that keeps generating alerts. What's the appropriate, targeted fix?

options.text: Add a narrow exclusion (e.g. that path) so scanning ignores the known fixture, without disabling scanning elsewhere
Disable secret scanning for the whole repository
Make the repository public
Delete the repository

questions.explanation: **Exclusions** let you tell scanning to ignore a specific known-false path, keeping coverage everywhere else. Disabling scanning repo-wide (b) blinds you to real secrets; making the repo public (c) exposes code and doesn't fix the noise; deleting the repo (d) is absurd. Keep exclusions narrow so you don't hide real leaks.

questions.stem: Which of the following are **true** about Secret Protection? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Push protection can be enabled for custom patterns, not just built-in ones
Closing a secret scanning alert does not revoke the underlying credential
Validity checks help prioritise alerts by confirming whether a secret is still active
On public repositories you must purchase GitHub Secret Protection before scanning starts

questions.explanation: (a), (b) and (c) are core, tested facts: custom patterns support push protection; closing an alert is bookkeeping (not rotation); and validity drives prioritisation. (d) is false — **public** repos are scanned automatically and free; the paid product is for **private/internal** repos.

questions.stem: An engineering manager argues: "We enabled push protection, so we don't need alerts or rotation — secrets can't get in." What's the flaw?

options.text: By default any writer can bypass push protection, and secrets predating it are already in history — so alerts and rotation are still essential
There's no flaw — push protection makes leaks impossible
Push protection also deletes historical secrets automatically
Alerts are only for public repositories, so enterprises don't need them

questions.explanation: Push protection is prevention, but it's **permissive by default** (any writer can bypass) and does nothing about secrets **already committed** before it was on — those need detection and **rotation**. So it doesn't make leaks impossible (b), it doesn't scrub history (c), and alerts apply to private/enterprise repos too (d). Prevention and detection are complementary, not either/or.

scenarios.title: A live cloud key just leaked
scenarios.tagline: Triage, remediate, and close out a real secret exposure in the right order.
scenarios.context: Secret scanning opens an alert on a private repo (Secret Protection is enabled): a cloud provider access key is hardcoded in a commit merged to `main` last night. You're the engineer on call. Work it end to end.

steps.prompt: First move: how do you gauge how urgent this alert is?

choices.text: Check the alert's validity status — if it's confirmed **active**, treat it as an exploitable, high-priority incident
choices.rationale: Validity is exactly the triage signal: an active credential is provably usable and jumps the queue.

choices.text: Sort all alerts by detection time and work the oldest first
choices.rationale: Priority is driven by validity/severity, not detection order — an old inactive secret is less urgent than a fresh active one.

choices.text: Assume it's fine because it's a private repo
choices.rationale: Private doesn't mean safe — a live key committed to a repo many people can read is a real exposure.

steps.prompt: Validity says **active**. What's the correct first *remediation* action?

choices.text: Revoke/rotate the key at the cloud provider — delete the old one and issue a new one
choices.rationale: The secret is compromised; killing the live credential at the source is the only thing that actually stops abuse.

choices.text: Force-push to remove the commit from history, then consider it handled
choices.rationale: Rewriting history doesn't un-expose a secret that may already be cloned or cached — it must be rotated.

choices.text: Close the secret scanning alert as 'revoked' so the dashboard is clean
choices.rationale: Closing the alert is bookkeeping and does not revoke anything — doing it first leaves the live key working.

steps.prompt: The key is rotated. What next, before you close anything?

choices.text: Update every service/pipeline that used the old key to the new one, then review provider logs for unauthorised use during exposure
choices.rationale: Rotation breaks anything still using the old key, and the logs tell you whether the window was abused — both are part of proper remediation.

choices.text: Immediately close the alert; log review can wait indefinitely
choices.rationale: Updating dependents is essential, but skipping the log review leaves you blind to whether the key was already misused.

choices.text: Add the key's file path to scanning exclusions so it stops alerting
choices.rationale: Excluding a path with a real secret hides genuine exposure — exclusions are for known-false fixtures, not live leaks.

steps.prompt: Everything's fixed and verified. How do you close out the alert?

choices.text: Close it with an accurate resolution (e.g. 'revoked') — as the final bookkeeping step now that the secret is actually rotated
choices.rationale: Now the alert state matches reality: the credential is dead, so recording 'revoked' is honest and correct.

choices.text: Leave it open forever as a reminder
choices.rationale: Not harmful, but a resolved exposure should be closed so the queue reflects true outstanding risk.

choices.text: Close it as 'false positive' to avoid questions
choices.rationale: It was a real, active leak — mislabelling it as a false positive corrupts your security record and hides a genuine incident.

scenarios.title: Roll out Secret Protection as a real gate
scenarios.tagline: Enable, prevent, customise, and control bypass across an org — without turning it into an honour system.
scenarios.context: You're the platform security lead enabling Secret Protection across an organisation's private repositories. You want prevention on by default, coverage for internal token formats, and bypass that's actually controlled.

steps.prompt: You need Secret Protection (with push protection) on across all current and future org repos. How do you enable it?

choices.text: Apply an organisation-level security configuration so repositories inherit Secret Protection and push protection
choices.rationale: Org-level configuration with inheritance covers existing and new repos in one move — the scalable control.

choices.text: Enable it repo by repo in each repository's settings
choices.rationale: It works today but doesn't cover repos created next month and doesn't scale to hundreds of repos.

choices.text: Convert the private repos to public so scanning is automatic
choices.rationale: Exposing proprietary code to save a licence is never the answer.

steps.prompt: Your company issues internal tokens GitHub doesn't recognise, and you want them blocked at the push. What do you set up?

choices.text: Create org-level custom patterns for the token formats, dry-run them, then enable push protection on the patterns
choices.rationale: Custom patterns extend detection to your formats, the dry run tunes out noise, and push protection on the pattern blocks them at the source.

choices.text: Enable push protection on the new custom patterns immediately, skipping the dry run
choices.rationale: You'll block the tokens, but an untested pattern may be noisy or over-broad and frustrate developers — dry-run first.

choices.text: Ask GitHub to add your internal format to its partner patterns
choices.rationale: Partner patterns are for GitHub's service-provider partners, not per-customer internal formats — that's what custom patterns are for.

steps.prompt: Right now any developer with write access can push past a block. You want only a trusted group to bypass directly, with everyone else reviewed. What do you configure?

choices.text: Delegated bypass — put trusted roles/teams on the bypass list; other contributors' requests go to a reviewer and expire in 7 days
choices.rationale: This is exactly delegated bypass: privileged actors bypass directly, everyone else is reviewed — turning the speed bump into a gate.

choices.text: Tell developers by policy not to bypass unless authorised
choices.rationale: An honour system isn't enforcement; the default still lets any writer bypass.

choices.text: Disable push protection so there's nothing to bypass
choices.rationale: Removing the control entirely is the opposite of tightening it.

steps.prompt: Finally, you're worried developers will quietly close real alerts as 'false positive' to hit deadlines. What control addresses that?

choices.text: Enable delegated alert dismissal so a security-manager team must approve alert closures
choices.rationale: Delegated dismissal adds a reviewer to the *alert-closing* path, stopping careless dismissal of genuine secrets.

choices.text: Rely on delegated bypass to also cover alert dismissal
choices.rationale: Bypass governs pushing past a block, not closing an alert — different control, different point.

choices.text: Configure alert recipients so more people are notified, and call it done
choices.rationale: Notifying more people helps visibility but doesn't *prevent* an unauthorised dismissal the way required review does.

labs.title: Enable push protection and watch it block a secret
labs.goal: Prove to yourself that push protection stops a secret at the push, and see exactly what the bypass flow records.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

Use a **throwaway test repository** (never a real secret — use a clearly fake token string in an obviously safe format for a provider you don't use).

1. **Enable it.** In the test repo, go to **Settings → Advanced Security** (or *Code security*) and turn on **secret scanning** and **push protection**. On a private repo this needs Secret Protection; on a public repo scanning is already on and you just enable push protection.
2. **Trigger a block.** Locally, add a line with a fake-but-realistic provider token to a file, commit it, and run:
   ```
   git push origin HEAD
   ```
   Push protection should **reject the push** and name the detected secret.
3. **Take the clean path.** Remove the token line, amend/commit, and push again — it should succeed. This is the intended "remove and re-push" flow.
4. **Explore the bypass (optional, understand the trail).** Re-introduce the fake token and push; when prompted, read the three bypass reasons. Note (without necessarily completing it) that **"I'll fix it later"** would leave an **open** alert while **"used in tests"/"false positive"** would leave a **closed** one.
5. **Confirm the record.** Open **Security → Secret scanning alerts** and see how the state reflects what you did.

## Why it matters

The exam tests prevention-vs-detection and the bypass outcomes. Feeling the block fire — and seeing that a bypass still leaves a tracked alert — makes those facts stick.
checklist: Push protection blocked a push containing a (fake) secret
Removing the secret and re-pushing succeeded
You can name the three bypass reasons and which leave an open vs closed alert
You located the secret scanning alerts view and saw the resulting alert state
You used only a fake token — never a real credential

labs.title: Author a custom pattern with a dry run, then arm push protection
labs.goal: Detect an org-specific token format that GitHub's built-in patterns miss, validate it safely, and block it at the push.
labs.steps:
## Steps

You'll need a repo (or org) with **Secret Protection / GHAS** enabled and admin rights.

1. **Invent a format.** Decide on a fake internal token shape, e.g. `ACME_` followed by 32 hex chars. A matching regex might be:
   ```
   ACME_[0-9a-f]{32}
   ```
2. **Create the pattern.** Go to **Settings → Advanced Security → Secret scanning → Custom patterns → New pattern**. Enter the **secret format** regex; optionally add before/after context or additional match requirements to reduce false positives.
3. **Dry run first.** Use the **dry run** to preview what the pattern would match across the repo/org. Refine the regex until the matches look right — this is how you avoid flooding the org with noise.
4. **Publish and (optionally) protect.** Save the pattern, then **enable push protection** on it so commits containing `ACME_...` tokens are blocked at the push, just like partner secrets.
5. **Verify.** In a test branch, add a fake `ACME_<32 hex>` string and push — push protection should block it. Remove it and re-push to confirm the clean path.

## Checkpoint

You've now extended both **detection** and **prevention** to a secret GitHub didn't know about — and you validated with a dry run before anyone got blocked.
checklist: Custom pattern created with a working secret-format regex
Dry run previewed matches and you refined to cut false positives
Push protection enabled on the custom pattern
A fake token in the custom format was blocked on push
You can state which levels (repo/org/enterprise) custom patterns can live at

resources.title: GH-500 Study Guide — Configure and use Secret Protection (task statements)
resources.annotation: The authoritative D2 task list: enable at repo/org, public vs private behaviour, push protection, validity checks & prioritised alerts, alert lifecycle, delegated bypass, alert recipients/exclusions, custom patterns. Skills measured as of July 2026 — the source of truth for what this domain tests.

resources.title: About secret scanning (GitHub Secret Protection)
resources.annotation: Extract the product-vs-engine naming (GitHub Secret Protection product / secret scanning engine), the public-free vs private-needs-Secret-Protection availability split, and partner vs non-provider vs custom pattern types.

resources.title: Secret scanning — introduction & enablement
resources.annotation: Extract how scanning works across full Git history on all branches, availability tiers (public/free, private via Secret Protection on Team/Enterprise Cloud, Enterprise Server), and where you enable it (repo settings, org security configuration, exclusions).

resources.title: About push protection
resources.annotation: Extract the prevention-at-the-push model, the remove-and-re-push path, the three bypass reasons and their alert outcomes (used in tests/false positive → closed; I'll fix it later → open), and the default 'any writer can bypass' behaviour plus who is notified on bypass.

resources.title: Push protection (concept) & custom-pattern push protection
resources.annotation: Extract the conceptual prevention-vs-detection contrast and the fact that push protection can be enabled for custom patterns, not just built-in ones.

resources.title: About validity checks for secret scanning
resources.annotation: Extract what a validity check does (verify active/inactive/unknown with the provider), how to enable it (repo/org/enterprise security settings), periodic + on-demand re-checks, and how confirmed-active secrets drive prioritised, high-confidence alerting.

resources.title: Resolving alerts from secret scanning
resources.annotation: Extract the remediation workflow (revoke/rotate the compromised token, create a new one, update services, check logs) and the key trap: secret scanning does NOT auto-close an alert when the token is removed — you close it manually, and closing is not revocation.

resources.title: About custom patterns
resources.annotation: Extract that custom patterns are regexes defined at repository/organisation/enterprise level, support additional match / must-not-match requirements, can have push protection enabled, and should be validated with a dry run before going live.

resources.title: Delegated bypass for push protection
resources.annotation: Extract who bypasses directly (bypass list + org owners + security managers) vs who is subject to a review cycle, that requests expire after 7 days, and the difference between bypass permission and full exemption from push protection.

resources.title: Enabling delegated alert dismissal for secret scanning
resources.annotation: Extract that delegated dismissal requires a security-manager team to approve alert closures (enabled at repo/org/enterprise via security configuration) — the alert-side mirror of delegated bypass — and the need for adequate reviewer coverage.

resources.title: MS Learn — Configure and use secret scanning in your GitHub repository
resources.annotation: The GH-500-aligned MS Learn module (GitHub Advanced Security Part 1). Work the 'What is / Configure / Use secret scanning' units and the exercise to practise enabling and using Secret Protection hands-on.

videos.title: D2 · Secret Protection — enable it & block at the push
